subj.title: Massive red, dead galaxy spotted in young universe

narrative:
Short impact summary

The research on a massive, quiescent galaxy at a redshift of 3.717 has garnered significant academic attention, as evidenced by its 244 citations across prominent journals. This work demonstrates a substantial impact within the astrophysics community, particularly through its citations in key journals such as The Astrophysical Journal and Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society.

Full narrative

The study detailing a massive, quiescent galaxy at a redshift of 3.717 has emerged as a significant contribution to the field of astrophysics, with a total of 244 citations noted in OpenAlex. This level of engagement indicates the research has resonated strongly within the academic community, facilitating discussions and advancing knowledge surrounding galaxy formation and evolution during the early universe.

Key institutions such as the Max Planck Institute for Astronomy and the Australian National University are among the affiliations of the authors, illustrating a collaborative effort that spans multiple prestigious organizations. This institutional backing underscores the credibility of the research, fostering further exploration and inquiry in the field.

Notably, the work has seen proven downstream reuse, enabling 245 subsequent research efforts that build upon its findings. This active utilization of the data highlights the scholarly value of the study and its foundational role as a dataset within the astrophysics community, facilitating continued discovery and knowledge sharing.

The citing landscape reflects a diverse international interest, with top citing countries including the United States, United Kingdom, and Germany, illustrating a broad geographical reach. This indicates that the study's findings are not only pertinent to a specific regional audience but are influential across a wide spectrum of researchers dedicated to understanding cosmic phenomena.
